Specific recognition strengthens the child’s understanding of their own character.
Strengths Can Also Create Difficulties
A strength is not automatically helpful in every situation.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card
Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

rows: Refusing to change an ineffective approach
Losing focus through constant new interests
Treating every activity as a contest

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card
Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: We do not need to remove the strength. We help the child use it wisely. For example:
“Your persistence is valuable. Wisdom also means recognising when a different method is needed.”
Or:
“You care deeply about your friends. You are still not responsible for solving every problem they have.”

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Maturity involves learning when, where and how to use one’s strengths.
Avoid Turning Strength into Pressure

The moment a child shows promise, adults often accelerate. A child enjoys drawing, so the parent enrols them in multiple classes. The child receives praise, so competitions begin. A social-media account is created. Every picture is evaluated. Soon the child no longer draws freely. What began as exploration becomes another performance system.
Signs that support may be becoming pressure

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: The parent is more invested than the child.
The child fears disappointing the coach, teacher or parent.
Every session is discussed in terms of results.
The child is not permitted to change direction.
School, sleep or relationships are suffering.
The activity has become part of the parent’s status.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: The child’s achievements are frequently displayed without consent.
The child has stopped enjoying the process.

blocks.text: Ask yourself:
“Am I supporting my child’s interest—or building my own project around it?”
That is not always a comfortable question. It is an important one.
Do Not Monetise Every Ability
In a world of online platforms and visible success stories, parents may quickly ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: Can we turn it into content?

blocks.text: Sometimes older children genuinely want to pursue these possibilities. But childhood also needs spaces where effort is not public, profitable or strategic. A child should be allowed to:

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: Learn something without adding it to a résumé

blocks.text: Joy has value even when it produces nothing marketable.
Helping a Child Who Says, “I’m Not Good at Anything”
Do not answer only with:
“Of course you are!”
The reassurance is loving but too general to change the child’s belief. Offer evidence.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: “I’ve noticed that younger children trust you because you explain things patiently.”
“When our travel plan changed, you adjusted faster than the rest of us.”
“You may not have found your favourite subject yet, but you are excellent at noticing how machines work.”

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Then create opportunities for the child to experience competence. Self-belief grows through evidence, not slogans alone. The American Academy of Pediatrics identifies meaningful competence, belonging, purpose and successful problem-solving as important foundations of healthy self-esteem. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Building Blocks for Healthy Self-Esteem](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/gradeschool/Pages/Helping-Your-Child-Develop-A-Healthy-Sense-of-Self-Esteem.aspx)

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: If a child persistently describes themselves as worthless, withdraws, gives up without trying or loses interest in usual activities, look beyond talent development. These can be signs of low self-esteem or emotional difficulty that deserve attention. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Signs of Low Self-Esteem](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/gradeschool/Pages/Signs-of-Low-Self-Esteem.aspx)
When Your Child’s Strength Is Not the One You Expected

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Parents carry dreams. Perhaps you imagined a child who loved books. Your child wants to build things. You value public achievement. Your child is happiest contributing quietly. You hoped for competitive sport. Your child loves theatre. You planned a traditional profession. Your teenager is interested in a path you do not yet understand. Parents do not have to approve every passing idea or abandon practical thinking. But before dismissing an interest, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: What does my child find meaningful here?
What abilities does this activity use?
What does a realistic path look like?
What education or training may be required?
Can the child meet someone with genuine experience in the field?
What are the risks, costs and opportunities?
Is this an interest, a developing strength or a serious long-term goal?
How can we explore it responsibly before making major decisions?

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Curiosity is more useful than ridicule. Your child should not have to become a copy of you in order to earn your respect.
Creating a Balanced Opportunity Plan
A child does not need ten activities. A balanced plan might include:
One activity for competence
Something the child wants to practise and improve.
One activity for physical wellbeing
Movement, sport, dance, walking, cycling or another suitable form of activity.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: One activity for joy or rest
Something without constant evaluation.
One contribution
A meaningful responsibility at home, school or in the community. These categories may overlap. The right balance depends on:

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: The needs of the whole family

blocks.text: A child’s development should not bankrupt or exhaust the household. Opportunity matters. So does sustainability.
Common Mistakes
1. Naming the child too early

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: “You are our athlete.” “You are the academic one.” “You are the artistic child.” Labels can become cages. Allow identities to remain flexible.
2. Valuing only visible achievement
Awards are evidence of performance in one setting—not the only proof of strength.
3. Choosing activities for status
A prestigious activity that the child dislikes may offer less development than a modest one they genuinely engage with.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: 4. Comparing siblings’ strengths
One child’s ability does not reduce another’s. Avoid turning strengths into family rankings.
5. Confusing early success with destiny
Some children develop quickly and later lose interest. Others begin slowly and grow through sustained practice.
6. Rescuing the child from beginner discomfort
Developing a strength includes awkwardness, mistakes and frustration.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: 7. Overscheduling
A timetable full of “enrichment” may leave no space for curiosity, rest or self-directed exploration.
8. Ignoring character
Talent without responsibility, humility or empathy does not produce a well-prepared adult.
9. Treating every hobby as career preparation
Some activities are valuable because they make life richer.
10. Speaking for the child

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Let older children communicate with teachers, coaches and mentors increasingly on their own.
Red-Flag Warnings
Pause and reassess when:

items: A coach or teacher humiliates children to improve performance.
The activity demands unsafe dieting, training or physical risk.
The child is pressured to hide pain or injury.
An adult requests secrecy or inappropriate private contact.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: The child’s image or work is shared publicly without suitable consent and safeguards.
Financial demands escalate without clear explanation.
The programme promises fame, admission or professional success.
Sleep and school functioning are consistently deteriorating.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: The child shows panic, persistent distress or loss of enjoyment.
The child’s worth is being linked to selection, ranking or income.
Adults discourage parents from observing or asking questions.
The child is not permitted to stop an activity despite harm.

blocks.text: Talent development never requires abandoning safety.
Your Child’s Strengths Map
Complete this over several weeks rather than in one sitting.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: What does my child choose to do voluntarily?
What do they learn quickly?
What will they practise without much prompting?
What kind of problems do they enjoy solving?

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: What do other people ask them for help with?
When have I seen them persist?
When have I seen them show empathy, honesty or courage?
In which environment do they seem most confident?

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Which activities create energy?
Which activities consistently drain them?
What opportunity could we offer next?
What meaningful responsibility could they take on?

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: How will we support this without taking ownership?
The Opportunity Review
Before committing to a new class or programme, ask:

items: Does my child want to explore this?
What is the purpose—joy, skill, health, community or competition?
Is the teacher or organisation safe and credible?

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: Can the child try it before making a long commitment?
How will we decide whether to continue?
Are we comfortable with photography, social media and communication policies?
Is there room for the child to remain a beginner?

blocks.text: Five Sentences to Practise

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: “I noticed how patiently you kept trying different solutions.”
“You do not have to turn everything you enjoy into an achievement.”
“Would you like help developing this, or would you prefer to keep it as something you do for fun?”
“Your marks tell us something about school; they do not tell us everything about you.”
“Let’s explore this interest before deciding what it must become.”

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: A strength grows through use, feedback, opportunity and time. Your role is not to predict your child’s final destination. It is to help them:

items: Build a life larger than any single measure of success

blocks.text: Try This Today
For one day, listen for a strength that would never appear on a report card. Perhaps your child:

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

items: Makes a younger child feel included

blocks.text: Describe exactly what you observed. Say:
“When the younger children disagreed, you listened to both of them and found a fair solution. That showed patience and leadership.”
Then stop. Do not add:
“You should become a lawyer.”

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Let the child receive the observation without immediately carrying a future.
From Mom’s Village

Sometimes a child’s abilities arrive in forms a family recognises immediately. Sometimes they arrive quietly. They may appear in a repaired appliance, an invented story, a kind gesture, a well-organised cupboard, a difficult question or the patience to sit beside someone who is hurting. Stay attentive. Your child may not yet have the words to name what they do well. For a while, they may borrow your eyes. Let those eyes be honest.

pages.chapterTitle: Discovering Strengths Beyond the Report Card

blocks.text: Do not exaggerate. Do not compare. Do not turn every possibility into pressure. Simply say:
“I see something in the way you do this.”

Then offer room, guidance and time. The purpose of discovering strengths is not to prove that your child is exceptional. It is to help your child understand where they can learn, contribute and grow with a genuine sense of competence. That understanding becomes part of the foundation from which independence is built.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children
pages.epigraph: “Growing older does not mean feeling less. It means gradually learning what to do with everything you feel.”

blocks.text: The Door That Slammed

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: The moment Anika entered the house, her mother knew something was wrong. Her schoolbag landed heavily on the floor. “How was your day?” her mother asked. “Fine.” “You don’t look fine.” “I said I’m fine.” Her mother followed her towards the bedroom. “Did something happen at school?” “No.” “Was it the mathematics test?” “Please stop asking me questions!” Anika slammed the door. Her mother’s concern became anger. “You do not speak to me like that,” she said, opening the door. “Come out immediately.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Anika began crying. Her mother continued: “You are fourteen years old. This behaviour is ridiculous.” What neither of them knew in that moment was that Anika’s closest friends had excluded her from a gathering and posted photographs where she could see them. She had held herself together throughout the school day. At home, where she felt safest, everything spilled out. The slammed door still needed to be addressed. But the door was not the whole story.
Anika was not simply being disrespectful. She was overwhelmed, hurt and ashamed—and she did not yet know how to say:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: “Something happened today, and I cannot talk about it without falling apart.”
Bigger Children Still Become Overwhelmed

As children grow, adults expect greater emotional control. That expectation is reasonable. A fourteen-year-old should gradually have more emotional skills than a four-year-old. But these skills do not appear automatically on a birthday. Adolescence brings:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: A still-developing capacity to manage intense emotion

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: A teenager may be articulate during an ordinary conversation and still lose access to that articulation when overwhelmed. The CDC notes that teenagers experience more fluctuations in mood than younger children and do not yet manage emotion as consistently as adults. Parents can help by observing, pausing, listening and coaching when everyone is calmer. [CDC: Coaching Teens to Recognise and Manage Emotions](https://www.cdc.gov/parenting-teens/tips-coaching-emotions/index.html)
This developmental understanding should create compassion. It should not remove boundaries.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Emotion and Behaviour Are Not the Same
Every emotion is allowed. Not every behaviour is. A child may feel:
The feeling itself is not misconduct. But the child may not:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Use intimidation to control the family

blocks.text: A parent can say:
“You are allowed to be furious. You are not allowed to hit your brother.”
Or:
“I understand that you felt excluded. Posting that photograph to embarrass your friend was still not acceptable.”
Validation means:
“Your emotional experience makes sense to me.”
It does not mean:
“Everything you did because of that emotion was acceptable.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Children need both understanding and accountability.
What Happens During Emotional Escalation?
When emotion becomes intense, the body prepares to respond. A child may experience:

items: Urge to escape, fight or shut down
Reduced ability to think through consequences

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: During high emotional arousal, reasoning becomes harder. This is why questions such as:

items: “Can’t you see how unreasonable you’re being?”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: often fail during the peak of an outburst. The child may genuinely be unable to offer a thoughtful answer in that moment. The American Academy of Pediatrics describes a useful sequence: adults first regulate themselves, then relate to the teenager’s experience, and only after calm has returned move towards reasoning. [American Academy of Pediatrics: What’s Going On in the Teenage Brain?](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/teen/Pages/Whats-Going-On-in-the-Teenage-Brain.aspx)
The lesson can wait. Safety cannot.
The CALMER Method

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Use this six-part method when emotions are rising.
C — Check Safety
Ask yourself:

items: Is anyone at risk of being hurt?
Is the child holding something dangerous?
Is the child trying to leave in an unsafe condition?
Are siblings frightened or being targeted?

blocks.text: If there is immediate danger:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Remove dangerous objects if this can be done safely.
Do not physically restrain an older child unless there is an immediate safety necessity and you are trained or directed to do so.

blocks.text: Safety comes before teaching, winning or preserving authority.
A — Attend to Your Own State
Before trying to calm your child, notice yourself.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Are you about to say something cruel?
Are you trying to regain control rather than solve the problem?

blocks.text: Pause. Lower your voice. Slow your movements. Stand at a respectful distance. You might say:
“I’m becoming too upset to handle this well. I’m going to pause, and I will come back.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: This is not surrender. It is responsible emotional control. The CDC encourages parents to pause before responding and to return to the conversation later if they are not yet calm enough to coach effectively. [CDC: Coaching Teens to Recognise and Manage Emotions](https://www.cdc.gov/parenting-teens/tips-coaching-emotions/index.html)
L — Lower the Intensity
During escalation:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Do not block the doorway unless safety requires it.
Offer water or a quieter place.

blocks.text: You may say:
“We’re both upset. We will talk after we’ve had time to settle.”
Or:
“You can sit here quietly or take space in your room. We will return to this.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Do not insist that a child use a calming technique simply because you suggested it. “Take a deep breath!” can sound like criticism when delivered angrily. Calming tools work best when practised before the crisis and chosen with the child.
M — Make the Child Feel Seen
Once the intensity begins to fall, use observation rather than accusation.
“You seem deeply disappointed.”
“That message really hurt you.”
“I think you felt humiliated when that happened.”
“You were trying to hold everything together, and then it became too much.”
If you are unsure, say:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: “I may be wrong. Help me understand when you’re ready.”
Avoid claiming certainty about the child’s inner experience. Being seen can reduce the need to demonstrate distress more loudly.
E — Explore When Calm
Later, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: “What did you notice in your body?”
“Is there something I should do differently?”

blocks.text: Do not conduct this conversation as a courtroom. The purpose is understanding, responsibility and future planning.
R — Repair and Rehearse
After calm:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Require apology or restitution where appropriate.
Practise a better sentence or action.

blocks.text: A child might rehearse:
“I’m too angry to talk. I need twenty minutes.”
The parent might rehearse:
“I can see this is not the time for questions. I’ll check on you later.”
Repair teaches that relationships can survive strong emotion—and that harm still carries responsibility.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Anger Is Often a Messenger
Anger can be the most visible emotion while another feeling remains underneath. Anger may protect:
A child may say:
“I hate that school!”
when the deeper truth is:
“I feel lonely there.”
Or:
“I don’t care about the result!”
when they mean:
“I am ashamed that I disappointed myself.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Do not insist that every anger must hide another emotion. Sometimes anger is simply anger. But curiosity can reveal what punishment alone cannot. Ask later:
“Was anger the whole feeling, or was something else underneath it?”
Shutdown Is Also an Emotional Response
Not every overwhelmed child shouts. Some become quiet. They may:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: A quiet child is not necessarily a calm child. Do not force immediate disclosure. Try:
“You don’t have to explain this now. I can see you’re affected. I’ll check on you after dinner.”
Or:
“Would you prefer company, space, or help finding words?”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: The child may communicate better:
The goal is not to make the child talk on demand. It is to keep a safe path back to communication.
Naming Emotions More Precisely
Children often rely on a few broad words:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: A richer emotional vocabulary helps them understand what they need. “Angry” may mean:
“Sad” may mean:
“Stressed” may mean:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: You can model precision:
“I’m not angry with you. I’m worried because I didn’t know where you were.”
“I felt embarrassed after I made that mistake, and I became defensive.”

The CDC recommends helping teens build language for emotions and observing clues in expression, voice and body language. [CDC: Coaching Teens to Recognise and Manage Emotions](https://www.cdc.gov/parenting-teens/tips-coaching-emotions/index.html)

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Build a Calm Plan Before It Is Needed
A calm plan should be created during a peaceful moment. Ask your child:
How do you know you are becoming overwhelmed?
Possible signs:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Urge to throw or hit something

blocks.text: What helps?
Possibilities include:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: What makes it worse?
Possibilities include:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: How will we reconnect?
Agree on a time or signal. For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: “If you ask for space, you may take thirty minutes. Then you will check in with me, even if you are not ready for the full conversation.”
Space without reconnection can become avoidance. Reconnection without space can become pursuit. The plan needs both.
Coping Tools Need Practice
Do not introduce every coping technique during a crisis. Practise when calm.
Slow breathing
The aim is a longer, slower exhale—not perfect counting. Try:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Grounding
Name:
This can help bring attention back to the present.
Physical movement

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Movement can release physical activation.
Writing
The child may write:

items: What they wish they could say

blocks.text: Sensory reduction

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: For some children, quiet, lower light, less conversation or a familiar object helps reduce overload.
Connection
A child may want:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics suggests acknowledging, naming and validating emotions while matching the response to the emotional need—for example, comfort for sadness, security for fear, and kindness with boundaries for anger. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Healthy Mental and Emotional Development](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/healthy-living/emotional-wellness/Building-Resilience/Pages/healthy-mental-and-emotional-development-in-children-key-building-blocks.aspx)
When Your Child Directs Anger at You
Teenagers may say:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: These words hurt. You do not have to pretend otherwise. But replying with equal cruelty makes repair harder. During escalation:
“I will not continue while we are speaking to each other this way.”
Later:
“You were extremely angry. Saying you hate me was hurtful. Tell me what you were trying to communicate.”
Teach an alternative:
“I am furious with your decision.”
“I feel controlled.”
“I need space before I can speak respectfully.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: The child may dislike your boundary. They still need a respectful way to express that dislike.
When You Lose Your Temper
Parents also become overwhelmed. You may shout, threaten or say something you regret. Repair honestly. A useful parental apology includes:

items: Stating what you will do differently

blocks.text: For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: “I shouted and called you ungrateful. That was disrespectful. I was angry, but my anger does not excuse those words. Next time I will pause before continuing. I am sorry.”
Avoid:
“I’m sorry, but you made me furious.”
That returns responsibility to the child. An apology does not weaken parental authority. It demonstrates the emotional responsibility you are asking your child to develop.
Common Mistakes
1. Reasoning during the peak
An overwhelmed brain is not ready for a lecture. Stabilise first.

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: 2. Matching the child’s intensity
Louder adult emotion usually produces more emotion, fear or shutdown.
3. Dismissing the feeling
Avoid:
A situation can seem small to you and still feel large to your child.
4. Accepting harmful behaviour because the child is upset

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Empathy does not replace accountability.
5. Forcing physical comfort
Ask before hugging an older child during distress.
6. Demanding immediate discussion
Some children need time before language returns.
7. Using the child’s vulnerability later
Never turn a private disclosure into a joke, public story or weapon during conflict.
8. Treating every mood change as pathology

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Ups and downs are part of development. Look for duration, severity and interference.
9. Treating persistent distress as “just hormones”
Development does not explain away suffering that is severe or impairing.
10. Making the parent the victim of every emotion
Your child’s difficult feelings are not automatically evidence that you have failed.
Red Flags: When Big Feelings Need More Support

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Consult a qualified healthcare or mental-health professional when emotional changes:

items: Interfere with school, sleep, eating, friendships or family life
Include repeated aggression or property destruction

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Are accompanied by an unhealthy relationship with food or exercise
Follow trauma, abuse, bullying, bereavement or another major event
Make the child feel out of control
Cause the family to feel unsafe

blocks.text: Seek urgent help if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: Talks about suicide or wanting to die
Talks about self-harm or has injured themselves
Threatens serious harm to another person
Is experiencing severe confusion or loss of contact with reality

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: UNICEF advises seeking professional help when concerning changes persist and interfere with everyday functioning, and urgently responding to self-harm or suicidal behaviour. [UNICEF: When to Help Your Teen Find Mental-Health Support](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/mental-health/when-help-your-teen-find-mental-health-support) Do not leave a child alone when there is an immediate risk of self-harm or suicide. Contact local emergency services or an urgent mental-health service.
Your Family Calm Plan

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: My child’s early signs of overwhelm
Situations that commonly trigger escalation
What helps my child calm safely
What usually makes things worse

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Words the parent can use
Words the child can use
Where the child may take safe space
How long before we check in?
Behaviours that remain unacceptable

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: What happens if safety is at risk?
How will repair happen afterwards?
Who can we contact for additional support?
The After-the-Storm Review
When everyone is calm, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: What happened before the emotion became intense?
What did you notice in your body?
What were you feeling besides anger?
What did you need in that moment?
Did anyone get hurt or frightened?
What could you say or do next time?
What should I do differently next time?

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Do not complete this review after every ordinary disagreement. Use it when a significant episode offers something important to learn.
Five Sentences to Practise

items: “Your feeling is allowed. This behaviour is not.”
“We do not have to solve this while we are both upset.”
“Would you like space, quiet company or help finding words?”
“I want to understand what happened underneath the anger.”
“We can repair this, and we still need to take responsibility for the harm.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Remember This
Calm is not the absence of emotion. It is the ability to remain safe and make increasingly thoughtful choices while emotion is present. Your child learns this through:

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

items: A parent who regulates before reacting
Coping tools practised in calm moments
Professional help when the difficulty is too large to manage alone

blocks.text: Try This Today
At a calm time, ask your child:
“When you’re overwhelmed, what do I do that helps—and what do I do that makes it worse?”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Listen without correcting their memory. Then agree on one sentence they can use during escalation. For example:
“I need twenty minutes. I promise I will come back.”
Agree on one sentence you will use:
“I’ll give you space, and I’ll check in at seven.”
Write both sentences somewhere private and easy to remember. A plan made during peace is easier to use during a storm.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: Big feelings can make a family wonder whether all its progress has disappeared. It has not. Emotional growth is rarely neat. Your child may handle disappointment wisely one week and slam a door the next. They may offer excellent advice to a friend and still struggle to use that advice themselves. This does not make the teaching pointless. It means the skill is still developing. Your job is not to prevent every storm. It is to help your child learn:
“I can feel this without becoming dangerous.”
“I can take space without disappearing from the relationship.”
“I can make a mistake and repair it.”

pages.chapterTitle: Big Feelings in Bigger Children

blocks.text: “I can ask for help before everything becomes unbearable.”
And perhaps most importantly:
“My feelings do not frighten the people who love me.”
When children trust that difficult emotions can be met with steadiness, boundaries and care, they gradually learn to offer those things to themselves.

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice
pages.epigraph: “One day, your voice will become part of the voice your child uses inside their own mind. Choose its words with care.”

blocks.text: The Audition

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: For weeks, Leena practised for the school play. She learnt every line. She rehearsed in front of the mirror and performed for anyone at home who would listen. On the morning of the audition, she was excited. That afternoon, she returned quietly. “What happened?” her father asked. “I wasn’t selected.” He wanted to make the pain disappear. “That teacher doesn’t recognise talent,” he said. “You were probably the best person there.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Leena shook her head. “I forgot part of my speech.” “That doesn’t matter. You’re still amazing.” Leena went to her room. Her father had tried to protect her confidence. But his reassurance did not match her experience. She had forgotten her lines. The disappointment was real. Later, her mother sat beside her. “That must hurt after all the work you did,” she said. Leena nodded. “Do you want me to listen, or do you want to think about what you might do differently next time?”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: “Just listen first.” So her mother listened. The next day, Leena said: “I think I practised the words, but I didn’t practise recovering when I made a mistake.” That insight did more for her confidence than being told she had been the best. She had learnt:
“I can face an honest result, understand it and grow.”
That is the kind of confidence that lasts.
Confidence and Self-Worth Are Related—but Different
Confidence
Confidence is the belief:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: “I can handle this task, learn this skill or face this situation.”

Confidence may vary from one area to another. A child can feel confident in sport and uncertain in mathematics. They may be relaxed with close friends and nervous in a large group. Confidence grows mainly through experience:
Self-worth
Self-worth is the deeper belief:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: “I have value as a person, even when I struggle, fail or disappoint someone.”
A child’s performance may change. Their human value does not. Healthy development needs both:
A child with confidence but fragile self-worth may appear successful while depending heavily on praise, ranking or approval. A child with stable self-worth can say:
“I did badly at this.”
without turning it into:
“I am bad.”
Healthy Confidence Is Not Constant Certainty

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: A confident child does not always feel brave. Healthy confidence may sound like:

items: “I don’t know how to do this yet.”
“I made a mistake and can correct it.”
“That person is better at this than I am, and I can still improve.”
“I was not chosen, and I will be all right.”
“I disagree, even if others do not approve.”
“I can leave a situation that is wrong for me.”

blocks.text: Confidence is not:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Believing you are better than everyone

blocks.text: Sometimes the most confident sentence is:
“I don’t understand. Please explain it again.”
Where the Inner Voice Comes From

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The inner voice is the running commentary a child carries about themselves. It may say:
Or it may say:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: “If I am not the best, I am nothing.”
“No one will like the real me.”

blocks.text: This voice is influenced by many things:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Parents do not control every influence. But they have a powerful one. Children notice not only how you speak to them, but also:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: A parent who regularly says:
“I’m useless. I can’t do anything right.”
may unintentionally teach a pattern of self-attack. A more useful model is:
“I made a mistake. I’m frustrated, and I’ll work out how to correct it.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The ROOTS of Healthy Self-Worth
Self-worth becomes more stable when it grows from several places rather than depending on one outcome. Use ROOTS as a guide.
R — Reliable Belonging
A child needs to know:
“I belong here even when I am difficult, unsuccessful or different.”
Reliable belonging does not mean approving every behaviour. It means discipline does not threaten the relationship. Avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: “No one will love you if you behave like this.”
“You are not the child I raised.”
Withdrawing affection after a poor result
Comparing the child unfavourably with a sibling

blocks.text: Use:
“I am unhappy with what you did. You are still my child, and we will work through it.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics identifies security and belonging as important foundations of healthy self-esteem. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Building Blocks for Healthy Self-Esteem](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/gradeschool/Pages/Helping-Your-Child-Develop-A-Healthy-Sense-of-Self-Esteem.aspx)
O — Opportunities for Competence
Confidence cannot be talked into existence. Children need to do real things. Give them opportunities to:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice
Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Meaningful contribution is especially powerful. UNICEF notes that helping family or community members and participating in activities that create mastery can provide teenagers with reliable sources of confidence. [UNICEF: Helping Your Teen Build Self-Confidence](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/mental-health/3-ways-help-your-teen-build-self-confidence)
O — Ownership
Let children increasingly own:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: If a parent constantly corrects, rescues and represents the child, the child may appear successful without feeling capable. Ownership includes safe consequences. A forgotten sports kit may produce inconvenience. An unfinished assignment may receive a natural school consequence. When parents prevent every discomfort, children lose opportunities to discover:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: “I can survive a mistake and respond to it.”
T — Truthful Feedback
Confidence built on exaggeration is fragile. Do not say:
“You were perfect.”
when the child knows they were not. Say:
“You spoke clearly, and you stayed with it after losing your place. The ending needs more practice.”
Useful feedback is:

items: Balanced enough to identify what is effective
Followed by a manageable next step

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The CDC recommends describing the particular behaviour being recognised rather than relying only on general praise. [CDC: Tips for Praising Your Teen](https://www.cdc.gov/parenting-teens/tips-praising-teens/index.html)
S — Self-Respect
Self-worth is not only feeling good. It is learning to act as though one has value. Self-respect includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Teach the child:
“You do not have to prove your worth by tolerating mistreatment.”
Praise That Builds Rather Than Controls

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Praise can strengthen connection and help children recognise useful behaviour. But praise becomes less helpful when it is:

items: Withheld whenever the child displeases the parent

blocks.text: Less useful

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: More useful

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: “You changed your method when the first one failed.”
“You told the truth even though you expected a consequence.”
“You included someone who was being left out.”
“You asked for feedback and used it.”

blocks.text: Specific praise helps the child recognise what they can repeat. But children also need interest without evaluation. Sometimes say:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Not every conversation needs a judgement.
Help the Child Build an Evidence-Based Inner Voice
Positive thinking should not require children to deny reality. If a child says:
“I’m terrible at mathematics.”
Do not immediately insist:
“No, you’re brilliant.”
The child may know that is untrue. Try:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: “Mathematics is difficult for you right now. Which part is most difficult?”
Then help create a more accurate sentence:
“I struggle with fractions, but I can improve with clear explanations and practice.”
Instead of:
“Everyone likes me.”
Try:
“Not everyone will choose me, and I can still find people with whom I belong.”
Instead of:
“I will definitely win.”
Try:
“I can prepare well, participate fully and handle the result.”
Instead of:
“I never make mistakes.”
Try:
“Mistakes are uncomfortable, and I know how to learn from them.”
The goal is not an endlessly positive inner voice. It is a fair, steady and useful one.

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The FACT Check for Harsh Self-Talk
Teach older children to examine a painful thought using FACT .
F — Find the Exact Thought
“Everyone thinks I’m stupid.”
A — Ask What Evidence Exists

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Is this a fact, prediction or feeling?
Am I using words such as always , never or no one ?
What would I say to a friend in this situation?

blocks.text: C — Create a More Complete Statement
“I answered one question incorrectly, and two students laughed. That was embarrassing. It does not prove everyone thinks I’m stupid.”
T — Take One Useful Step

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Prepare one question before the next class.

blocks.text: A thought does not need to become cheerful to become more truthful.
Confidence Grows Through Manageable Risk
Children need opportunities to do things that feel uncertain but are reasonably safe. Examples include:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Asking a shop assistant a question
Travelling a familiar route independently when ready
Speaking to a teacher about a concern
Submitting work that is good enough rather than perfect
Trying an activity where they may not excel

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The parent’s role is not to guarantee success. It is to help the child prepare, attempt and recover. Beforehand, ask:
“What are you worried might happen?”
“What can you control?”
“What will you do if it does not go as hoped?”
Afterwards, ask:
“What did you learn about yourself?”
Do not evaluate only the outcome. Courage deserves recognition even when selection does not follow.
Let Children Hear Their Own Voice
Parents often speak quickly when a child doubts themselves. Try asking questions instead:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: “What do you think you handled well?”
“What are you proud of that no one else noticed?”
“What advice would you give a friend?”
“What does this result not say about you?”

blocks.text: Self-evaluation is important. A child who depends entirely on adult praise may feel confident only when approval is present. We want the child gradually to say:
“I know what I did well.”
“I know where I need to improve.”
“I can respect myself through both.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Confidence and Appearance
Puberty can make the body feel unfamiliar. Children may compare:
Family comments carry particular weight. Avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Dividing foods into moral categories such as “good” and “bad”

blocks.text: Focus on:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: What the body allows the person to do

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: UNICEF advises parents to model respect for their own bodies, avoid criticising appearance and help teenagers value qualities beyond how they look. [UNICEF: Teens and Body Image](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/mental-health/teens-body-image) If appearance worries begin controlling food, exercise, social participation or everyday functioning, seek professional guidance.
The Role of Family Humour
Warm humour can bring families together. But repeated teasing may become part of a child’s inner voice. Be cautious with family labels such as:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Adults may insist:
“We’re only joking.”
The better question is:
“Does the child experience it as affection?”
If a child asks for a joke to stop, stop. Respecting that boundary teaches them that discomfort matters.
When Confidence Looks Like Arrogance

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Some children protect insecurity by appearing superior. They may:

items: Avoid situations where success is uncertain

blocks.text: Do not respond by humiliating them. Teach:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Confidence does not require another person to be smaller.
Other people’s success is not theft.
Respect is required regardless of skill.

blocks.text: Try:
“You are allowed to feel proud of your ability. Mocking someone who is learning is not acceptable.”
When Low Confidence Becomes a Family Pattern

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Sometimes a parent’s anxiety quietly shapes the child. A parent may:

items: Rescue at the first sign of distress
Avoid activities where the child may fail
Treat nervousness as evidence that the child cannot cope

blocks.text: The child may learn:
“If my parent is this worried, the world must be too difficult for me.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Supportive language sounds like:
“I know this feels uncomfortable. I also believe you can take the next step.”
Confidence grows when care and belief arrive together.
Common Mistakes
1. Trying to praise away pain
A disappointing event needs acknowledgement before encouragement.
2. Making love sound performance-based
Avoid reserving warmth for the moments when the child succeeds or behaves conveniently.

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: 3. Overprotecting the child from feedback
Children need respectful truth to develop genuine competence.
4. Correcting self-criticism with an argument
Curiosity is often more effective than contradiction.
5. Treating shyness as failure
A quiet temperament is not automatically low confidence. Ask whether the child is distressed or simply reserved.
6. Speaking negatively about yourself

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Children absorb the way adults judge their own bodies, mistakes and abilities.
7. Doing too much for the child
Repeated rescue can communicate, “You cannot manage without me.”
8. Confusing confidence with obedience
A confident child may question, disagree and hold a different opinion respectfully.
9. Publicising the child’s achievements without consent
Older children deserve a voice in what is shared about them.

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: 10. Expecting confidence to appear before experience
Confidence often follows action rather than preceding it.
Red Flags: When Low Self-Worth Needs More Support
Seek guidance from a qualified healthcare or mental-health professional when a child persistently:

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Says they are worthless, ugly, stupid or unwanted
Avoids ordinary challenges because failure feels unbearable
Withdraws from friends, family or activities
Cannot accept either praise or constructive feedback
Shows a major change in mood, sleep, appetite or functioning
Skips meals, overeats, purges or exercises compulsively
Remains in harmful relationships because they believe they deserve mistreatment

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: Uses substances to cope or fit in
Talks about self-harm, disappearing, death or suicide

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics lists withdrawal, harsh self-criticism, giving up without trying, sensitivity to others’ opinions and declining interest as possible signs of low self-esteem. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Signs of Low Self-Esteem](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/gradeschool/Pages/Signs-of-Low-Self-Esteem.aspx) Treat self-harm or suicidal statements seriously and seek urgent help when immediate safety may be at risk.
Your Child’s Inner-Voice Audit

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: What does my child say after making a mistake?
What do they say about their body?
What do they say when someone else succeeds?
What do they say before trying something difficult?

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: What labels do family members use about this child?
What do I say about myself after my own mistakes?
Does warmth change noticeably after success or failure?
Where does my child experience real competence?

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: What meaningful responsibility can they take on?
What manageable risk could they try next?
What sentence would I like them eventually to say to themselves?
A More Balanced Thought

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

rows: “This attempt did not work, and I can review why.”
“Other people have different strengths and experience.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

rows: “I feel rejected right now, but one event does not describe every relationship.”
“I am having a difficult day with my appearance; my body is not my whole value.”
“I need to meet the purpose of the task, not eliminate every flaw.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

rows: “I can take one step and ask for help if needed.”
“People may be disappointed by an outcome and still love me.”

blocks.text: Five Sentences to Practise

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

items: “This result tells us how something went; it does not tell us what you are worth.”
“I will not pretend it was perfect. I will help you see what worked and what can improve.”
“You can feel nervous and still take the next step.”
“What are you proud of that nobody else may have noticed?”
“You do not need to make someone else smaller in order to feel strong.”

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Self-worth is the ground. Confidence is what the child builds upon it. Strong foundations come from:

items: A family voice that is firm without becoming cruel

blocks.text: Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: Listen closely to the language your child uses after a mistake. Do not immediately correct it. If they say:
“I’m so stupid.”
Ask:
“What happened that made you speak to yourself that way?”
Then help create one sentence that is both kinder and more accurate:
“I misunderstood this question. I need to learn the method and try again.”
Write the sentence down if your child is willing. We are not teaching children to praise themselves falsely. We are teaching them not to become their own bully.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: You cannot protect your child from every rejection, mistake or unkind opinion. Nor should confidence depend on you successfully doing so. What you can offer is something steadier. A home where truth does not become humiliation. A relationship where failure does not threaten belonging. Opportunities to discover:
“I can learn.”
“I can contribute.”
“I can recover.”
“I can respect myself even when someone else does not choose me.”

pages.chapterTitle: Confidence, Self-Worth and the Inner Voice

blocks.text: For years, your child will borrow your belief. Lend it generously—but not blindly. Let it be a belief strong enough to see both their ability and their unfinished work. Then, little by little, help them make it their own. The goal is not a child who walks through life feeling superior or certain. It is a young person who can enter an uncertain world and say:
“I may not know exactly how this will go. But I know I can meet myself with honesty, respect and courage along the way.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure
pages.epigraph: “As children grow, friendship becomes one of the places where they discover who they are—and one of the places where they may be most afraid of losing themselves.”

blocks.text: The Empty Seat

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: For nearly two years, Tara and Sana had been inseparable. They sat together at lunch, attended the same dance class and spent most weekends at each other’s homes. Then a new group formed. The change was quiet at first. Messages were answered more slowly. Plans were discussed when Tara was close enough to hear but not included. At lunchtime, a bag began appearing on the empty seat beside Sana. One afternoon, Tara came home and said:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: “I don’t want to go to school tomorrow.” Her mother immediately became angry. “After everything you’ve done for Sana! Give me her mother’s number. I’ll speak to her.” “No!” Tara cried. “Please don’t do anything.” Her mother felt helpless. She wanted to defend her child. But Tara was afraid that adult intervention would make the exclusion more visible and humiliating. So her mother paused. She said:
“I won’t contact anyone tonight. Tell me what has been happening, and we’ll decide together what needs to happen next.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Tara did not need her mother to pretend the friendship was fine. She also did not need her mother to take over before understanding it. She needed a safe place to tell the whole story.
Why Friendship Matters So Much
As children move towards adolescence, friendships take on new importance. Friends provide opportunities to learn:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Friendships can be deeply protective. A good friend can help a child:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Peer influence is not automatically harmful. Young people also influence each other towards:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics notes that peer support can be invaluable and that friendships help teenagers develop important social abilities through real interaction. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Supporting Friendships](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/family-life/power-of-play/Pages/What-Parents-Can-Do-to-Support-Friendships.aspx) The challenge is not to remove peer influence. It is to help children recognise which influence strengthens them and which requires them to surrender safety, values or self-respect.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Friendships Change as Children Grow
Approximately 7–10 years
Friendships may centre on:
Disagreements may be intense but brief. A child may declare:
“We are never speaking again.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: and play with the same friend the next morning. Parents can help by teaching simple conflict skills without treating every disagreement as a permanent friendship crisis.
Approximately 11–14 years
Belonging often becomes more emotionally charged. Children may become highly aware of:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Friend groups may shift quickly. Exclusion can feel devastating because the child is developing identity partly through the group.
Approximately 15–18 years

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Friendships may become more stable, emotionally intimate and connected to values or future goals. However, older teenagers may also experience:

items: Pressure around substances, sex or risk
Conflict between family and peer expectations
Fear of leaving a group that has become unhealthy

blocks.text: The child may look almost adult while still needing support with judgement and boundaries.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Healthy Friendship, Difficult Friendship or Harmful Pattern?

Not every imperfect friendship is unhealthy. Friends disappoint one another. They misunderstand. They become jealous. They sometimes speak carelessly. The question is whether the relationship can contain honesty, respect and repair.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

rows: Both people can have other friends
Jealousy appears but can be discussed
One person controls whom the other may see
Conflict becomes heated but apologies occur
Disagreement leads to threats, punishment or humiliation
A confidence is mishandled once and repaired
Secrets are repeatedly shared or used as weapons

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

rows: The friendship feels uneven during a difficult period
One person consistently uses the other
Boundaries are mocked, ignored or punished

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

rows: The child sometimes feels pressure to fit in
Belonging depends on fear, imitation or obedience

blocks.text: Teach your child to look for patterns rather than one isolated event.
The FRIEND Check

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: When your child is uncertain about a friendship, use FRIEND .
F — Free to Be Yourself
Ask:

items: Can you express a different opinion?
Can you spend time with other people?
Do you feel you must perform a version of yourself to remain accepted?

blocks.text: Belonging that requires constant self-erasure is not secure belonging.
R — Respect Goes Both Ways
Respect includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: No pressure to share private information
No mocking of family, body, identity or background
No use of passwords as proof of loyalty
No expectation of immediate replies at all hours

blocks.text: I — Influence Is Safe
Ask:
“Who do you become when you spend time with this person?”
Does the friendship encourage:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Or does it repeatedly lead towards:
No friend determines every choice. But patterns of influence matter.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: E — Effort Is Mutual
One person should not always:
Friendships are not perfectly balanced every day. Over time, however, both people should matter.
N — No Fear Is Required
A child should not remain in a friendship because they fear:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Fear changes the relationship from friendship to control.
D — Difficulties Can Be Discussed
A friendship becomes more secure when both people can say:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Repair is one of the clearest signs of relational maturity.
How Parents Can Listen Without Taking Over
When your child describes friendship pain, resist the urge to solve it in the first minute. Begin with:
“That sounds painful.”
Then ask:
“Do you want me to listen, help you think through options, or step in?”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Your child may want different things at different times.
Listen
Useful when the child needs emotional safety and is not in danger.
Coach
Useful when the child needs help finding words, considering options or understanding another perspective.
Monitor
Useful when you are concerned but do not yet have enough information to intervene.
Intervene
Necessary when there is:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: A child unable to protect themselves

blocks.text: A parent should respect privacy. A parent should not promise secrecy when safety is at risk.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Friendship Conflict Without Parental Escalation
A disagreement between children can quickly become a conflict between families. Before contacting another parent, teacher or school, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Is this a disagreement, exclusion, bullying or danger?
Is there a repeated power imbalance?
Has my child tried a reasonable direct response?
What outcome does my child want?
Could adult contact increase humiliation or retaliation?
Can I involve my child in deciding how we proceed?
Is immediate action required for safety?

blocks.text: Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Confront a child directly without appropriate adult involvement
Demand that your child restore a friendship
Treat your child’s account as unimportant
Promise an outcome you cannot control

blocks.text: You may believe the other child behaved badly. Still avoid:
“She is a horrible person. I always knew it.”
Your child may reconcile with that friend and then stop confiding in you because they fear your judgement. Focus on behaviour:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: “Excluding you and then mocking your reaction was unkind. Let’s decide how you want to respond.”
Teach Direct but Respectful Communication
Children need words for ordinary friendship problems.
When feeling excluded
“I heard everyone discussing the plan. Was I intentionally not included?”
When hurt by a joke
“You may have meant it as a joke, but I felt humiliated. Don’t repeat it.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: When a confidence is shared
“I told you that privately. I need to understand why you shared it.”
When needing space
“I’m too upset to talk properly today. I need some time.”
When repairing
“I was angry, but what I posted was unfair. I’ve removed it, and I’m sorry.”
When ending a friendship
“This friendship no longer feels respectful to me. I’m stepping back.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Not every message needs to be delivered perfectly. The goal is to replace silence, explosion or online retaliation with clearer communication.
Exclusion, Rejection and the Friendship Break-Up
Friendship loss can feel like grief. A child may lose:
Avoid saying:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Those statements may eventually contain truth. They arrive too early. Try:
“You shared a lot with this person. It makes sense that losing the friendship hurts.”
Then help the child decide:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Does the friendship need a conversation?
Is an apology or repair possible?
Does the child need support entering another social setting?
Is online contact keeping the wound open?

blocks.text: Do not force a quick replacement friendship. Belonging takes time.
Helping a Child Make Friends
Some children need more deliberate support. They may be:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Practical support may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Inviting one child rather than a large group
Connecting through sport, art, service or community activities
Asking a teacher which peers may be a good match
Seeking social-skills or professional support when difficulty is persistent

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics suggests structured, low-pressure activities for children who find social interaction difficult and recommends interest-based groups as places to meet peers with something already in common. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Making Friends in High School](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/teen/school/Pages/Making-Friends-in-High-School.aspx) Do not announce:
“My child has no friends.”
Speak privately and respectfully.
Peer Pressure Is Often Subtle
Peer pressure is not always someone saying:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: “Do this or we won’t be your friends.”
It may sound like:

items: “If you cared about me, you would.”
“We all agreed—why are you being difficult?”

blocks.text: Pressure can involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Children should not have to invent a response for the first time while a group is watching. Prepare in advance.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: The EXIT Plan for Social Pressure
E — Explain Less
A child does not owe peers a long defence.
“No, I’m not doing that.”
“That doesn’t work for me.”
“I’m leaving.”
X — Use an Excuse if Needed
Purity is less important than safety. Allow the child to blame you:
“My parents check.”
“My mother will come looking for me.”
“I have to be home now.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends giving young people face-saving ways to avoid unsafe peer pressure, including allowing parents to be the reason they cannot participate. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Supporting Friendships](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/family-life/power-of-play/Pages/What-Parents-Can-Do-to-Support-Friendships.aspx)
I — Initiate Contact
Create a code word or ordinary-looking message. For example:
“Did you feed the dog?”
may mean:
“Call me and give me a reason to leave.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: The family should know exactly what action follows.
T — Take the Safe Way Out
Teach:

items: Never get into a vehicle with an impaired driver.
Never leave an unsafe friend behind if urgent adult help is needed.
Move towards a public or supervised place.
Contact emergency services when someone is in immediate danger.

blocks.text: Make this promise:
“If you call because you are unsafe, I will help first. We will discuss decisions later.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: UNICEF advises parents to plan for risky social situations before they occur and to assure teenagers that seeking adult help for safety will not become something they regret. [UNICEF: Teens and Risky Behaviour](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/mental-health/teens-risky-behaviours) This does not eliminate later accountability. It removes punishment as a barrier to emergency help.
Know the Friends Without Running the Friendship
Parental monitoring is not the same as constant surveillance. Know, in age-appropriate ways:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Who your child spends time with
Whether an adult will be present
What to do if plans change

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Get to know other parents when possible. Make your home a reasonably welcoming place. Observe the group without interrogating everyone. The CDC reports that parental monitoring works best within a caring, open relationship in which teenagers believe their parents can listen, be trusted and provide useful guidance. [CDC: Parental Monitoring](https://www.cdc.gov/healthy-youth-parent-resources/positive-parental-practices/parental-monitoring.html)
When You Dislike a Friend
Before banning a friendship, identify the concern. Is it:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Appearance, class, caste, religion, race or another bias?
Fear that your child is changing?

blocks.text: If the concern is serious, state it with evidence:
“On three occasions, you returned from this group after drinking. Until we have a safer plan and trust is rebuilt, unsupervised outings with them will stop.”
Avoid vague attacks:
“Those children are bad.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: A direct ban may sometimes be necessary for safety. But when danger is not immediate, harsh condemnation can drive the friendship underground. Ask questions that strengthen your child’s judgement:

items: “How do you feel after spending time with them?”
“Do you become more or less honest around this group?”
“What would they do if you needed help?”
“Can you say no without being punished?”

blocks.text: Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: 1. Taking every conflict personally
Your child’s friendship pain is not a verdict on your parenting.
2. Contacting other families while angry
Pause, gather information and decide what outcome is needed.
3. Insulting the friend
Criticise harmful behaviour rather than defining the entire child.
4. Forcing friendship
Children do not have to remain close because families are close.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: 5. Dismissing online friendship
Online relationships can be meaningful and can also carry risks. Understand the actual relationship.
6. Treating popularity as wellbeing
A child may know many people and have no safe, trustworthy friendship.
7. Treating solitude as failure
Some children need fewer friendships and more time alone. Ask whether the child is content or lonely.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: 8. Expecting the child to “just stand up for themselves”
Social pressure can be powerful. Practise words and exit plans.
9. Removing all social access as routine punishment
For older children, social connection matters greatly. Use proportionate consequences and preserve healthy relationships where possible.
10. Sharing private friendship details
Do not turn your child’s disclosures into family conversation or gossip.

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Red Flags: When Parents Should Step In
Intervene or seek appropriate school/professional help when you notice:

items: A friend threatening self-harm to prevent separation

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: Major changes in sleep, appetite, mood or functioning
The child giving money or possessions under pressure
The child believing they cannot leave the friendship safely
Any mention of self-harm or suicide

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Do not ask a child to manage serious coercion alone. Save relevant messages or screenshots when digital evidence may be needed. Avoid forwarding intimate material. Report through the appropriate school, platform, safeguarding, law-enforcement or child-protection route depending on the situation. Chapter 18 will address bullying, cruelty and online harm in greater depth.
Your Child’s Friendship Map
People my child feels safe with

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: People my child enjoys but does not fully trust
Relationships that often create distress
Adults outside the home my child can approach
Groups or activities where my child feels they belong

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Does my child have at least one relationship where they can be themselves?
Can my child disagree with friends?
Can my child say no?
Does my child know how to leave an unsafe situation?

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: What social opportunity might help?
What concern needs monitoring?
What concern requires intervention now?
The Friendship Reflection for Children and Teens
You may invite your child to consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

items: How do I usually feel after spending time with this person?
Can I be honest with them?
What happens when I say no?
Do I feel respected in person and online?
Do I behave in ways I respect when I am with them?
Can I ask them for help?
Would I want someone I care about to be treated this way?

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: The child does not need to show you every answer. The exercise is intended to strengthen their judgement.
Five Sentences to Practise

items: “Do you want me to listen, help you think, or step in?”
“You can care about a friend and still set a boundary.”
“Belonging should not require you to become unsafe or unkind.”
“If you call me from an unsafe situation, I will help first and discuss it later.”
“I will respect your privacy unless someone’s safety is at risk.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Remember This
The goal is not to choose every friend for your child. It is to help your child become someone who can recognise respect, practise it and leave when it disappears. Children need:

items: Adult intervention when harm exceeds what a child can manage

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: Try This Today
Ask your child one question:
“What do you think makes someone a safe friend?”
Do not begin by listing your own rules. Listen for what your child already understands. Then offer one thought of your own:
“I think a safe friend lets you say no without making you afraid.”
Keep the conversation brief. This is not a test of whether your child has chosen the “right” people. It is practice in recognising the qualities that healthy belonging requires.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: There may come a time when your child’s friends know things before you do. That can feel like a loss. It is also part of growing up. Your role is changing from being the centre of your child’s social world to being the secure place from which they enter it. Remain interested. Know where they are. Hold safety boundaries. Welcome their friends without pretending every friendship is wise. Listen when hearts break. Intervene when harm becomes too large.
And keep teaching this quiet truth:
“You do not have to earn belonging by abandoning yourself.”

pages.chapterTitle: Friendships, Belonging and Social Pressure

blocks.text: A child who carries that truth is better prepared not only to choose good friends, but also to become one.

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm
pages.epigraph: “A child should never have to become smaller, quieter or invisible in order to feel safe.”

blocks.text: The Photograph in the Class Group

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: When Rohan stopped asking for lunch money, his parents assumed he had begun carrying food from home. When he started taking a longer route to school, they thought he wanted extra time with friends. When his marks fell, his father removed his gaming privileges. No one knew that three older students had been waiting near the school gate. At first, they mocked his accent. Then they began taking his lunch money. One afternoon, they pushed him into a muddy patch, photographed him and shared the picture in a class group.

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Rohan did not tell his parents. He feared his father would ask why he had not fought back. He feared his mother would arrive at school and make everyone know. Mostly, he felt ashamed. The truth came out when his younger sister saw the photograph on another child’s phone. His mother’s first words were:
“I’m glad we know now. This is not your fault, and you will not handle it alone.”
Those words did not erase the harm. They removed one burden Rohan had been carrying—the belief that stopping it was entirely his responsibility.
Conflict, Cruelty and Bullying

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: These problems need different responses.
Conflict
Conflict usually involves children with relatively equal power who disagree or hurt one another. Examples include:
Conflict may require communication, boundaries, apology and repair.
Cruel behaviour

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: A single action can be deliberately hurtful even when there is no repeated pattern. Examples include:
One incident can still cause serious harm.
Bullying
Bullying generally involves:

items: A real or perceived power imbalance
Repetition, or a strong potential for repetition

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Power may come from size, age, popularity, group numbers, money, private information, authority or technology. Bullying can be:

items: Physical: hitting, pushing, blocking movement, stealing or damaging belongings
Verbal: threats, slurs, mocking and repeated insults
Social: rumours, deliberate isolation and public humiliation
Online: abusive messages, impersonation, embarrassing images, doxing, threats or non-consensual intimate content

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics emphasises that bullying is not simply “children being children”; it can affect education, sleep, mental health and long-term wellbeing. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Why Bullying Hurts](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/safety-prevention/at-play/Pages/Bullying-Its-Not-Ok.aspx) Do not become so focused on choosing the correct label that you fail to respond to the harm.
Why Children Stay Silent
A child may not tell because they:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Believe adults will make it worse
