Participated initially and later became a target
Broke a family rule before the incident

blocks.text: Tell your child before a crisis happens:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: “If something unsafe happens, you can tell me even if you broke a rule. We may discuss the rule later, but I will help with safety first.”

Do not automatically remove all digital access after disclosure. Children may experience this as punishment and become less willing to report future harm. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Cyberbullying](https://www.healthychildren.org/english/family-life/media/pages/cyberbullying.aspx) Temporary restrictions may be needed for safety, but explain their purpose.
Possible Warning Signs
A targeted child may show:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm
Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: These signs can have other causes. Ask without accusation:
“I’ve noticed that you become upset after checking the class group. Is someone making you feel unsafe or treating you badly?”
The SAFER Response
S — Secure Immediate Safety
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Is there stalking, extortion or sexual pressure?
Is the child afraid to attend school?
Has the child mentioned self-harm or suicide?

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: For immediate danger in India, contact 112 . Children in crisis can access Child Helpline 1098 , operated under Mission Vatsalya. [Government of India: Child Helpline 1098](https://spniwcd.wcd.gov.in/child-helpline) Do not send a child back into known danger while adults merely “wait and see.”
A — Affirm and Listen
Say:

items: “I’ll explain what I need to do next.”

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Avoid beginning with:

items: “What did you do to cause it?”

blocks.text: Safety and trust come first.
F — Fix the Evidence Safely
For ordinary abusive content:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Do not repeatedly make the child view the material, and do not circulate it among relatives or other parents.
If intimate images of a child are involved

Do not forward, repost or create additional copies. Preserve identifying information such as usernames, links and dates where possible without redistributing the image. Report urgently through the platform and appropriate child-protection or police route. Do not negotiate privately with the person sharing or threatening to share it.

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: E — Escalate Appropriately
Depending on the situation, report to:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: India’s [National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal](https://www.cybercrime.gov.in/) includes reporting routes for cybercrimes against women and children. Immediate danger, sexual exploitation, stalking or credible threats should not wait for an ordinary school process.
R — Restore Safety and Wellbeing
The child may need:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Ask:
“What would help you feel safer tomorrow?”
Then explain what adults will do.
Reporting to the School
Put serious concerns in writing. Include:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Effect on attendance, safety or wellbeing

blocks.text: A useful message may say:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: “We are reporting repeated unwanted behaviour involving a power imbalance, including threats and humiliating online posts. Our child is now afraid to attend school. Please confirm the immediate safety plan, the person responsible for follow-up and the date for review.”

Focus on stopping the behaviour and protecting your child rather than demanding confidential details about another child’s punishment. Follow up if the behaviour continues. Bullying may become less visible without actually stopping.
Do Not Force Mediation

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Conflict mediation assumes both children can negotiate safely. Bullying involves unequal power. Forced face-to-face mediation may:

items: Pressure the targeted child to forgive

blocks.text: A restorative conversation may sometimes help, but only with qualified facilitation, genuine accountability and the targeted child’s safe, voluntary participation. Protection must not depend on reconciliation.
What Children Can Practise

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: When safe and realistic:
Stop
“Enough.”
“Do not touch me.”
Walk
Move towards a supervised area, safe peers or a trusted adult.
Tell

Report to an adult who can act. Seeking help is not weakness or tattling. It is a safety skill. Do not make fighting the primary solution. Retaliation can increase danger and disciplinary consequences. Protecting oneself during an immediate attack is different from planning revenge.

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Responding to Online Harm

items: Change passwords if compromise is suspected.
Inform the school if peers are involved.
Escalate threats, stalking, extortion or sexual content.

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Documentation and reporting help schools, platforms and authorities respond. [StopBullying.gov: Report Cyberbullying](https://www.stopbullying.gov/cyberbullying/how-to-report) Never ask the child to keep engaging with an aggressor merely to collect more evidence.
When Your Child Has Caused Harm
Parents may feel denial, anger or shame. Begin with:
“I love you, and I need the truth. If you harmed someone, we will take responsibility.”
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Did you post, like, forward or encourage the content?
Did you know the person wanted it to stop?

blocks.text: “Everyone was doing it” does not remove responsibility.
Accountability may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Removal of harmful content where possible

blocks.text: Say:
“What you did was bullying.”
Avoid making the behaviour a permanent identity:
“You are an evil bully.”
The child must change the behaviour without being taught that change is impossible.
Repair Is More Than “Sorry”

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Meaningful repair may include:

items: Harmful content is removed where possible.
The child names what they did without excuses.
The targeted child is not forced to respond.

blocks.text: A better apology is:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: “I shared the photograph to embarrass you. That was cruel. I removed it and reported the copies I know about. I understand that deleting it does not erase the harm. I am sorry.”
Not:
“I’m sorry if you were offended.”
Teach Bystanders to Act Safely
Children watching cruelty can:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Refuse to laugh, like or forward
Seek urgent help if danger is present

blocks.text: A child does not have to place themselves in physical danger to help.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: Telling the child to ignore repeated aggression
Blaming the child for being targeted
Removing the device as punishment for disclosure
Publicly shaming a child who caused harm
Assuming your child could never participate in cruelty

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Urgent Red Flags
Seek immediate help for:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: A child who cannot safely return home or attend school

blocks.text: In India:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: These details should be verified again immediately before publication because services and procedures can change. This chapter provides general safety information, not legal advice.
Five Sentences to Practise

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: “I believe you, and you will not handle this alone.”
“You will not lose my help because you broke a rule before telling me.”
“We will preserve the evidence without spreading the harm.”
“I love you, and if you harmed someone, we will take responsibility.”
“Getting adult help is not tattling when someone is being harmed.”

blocks.text: Remember This
Bullying survives through fear, silence, power and an audience. Adults weaken it when they:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Try This Today
Ask your child:

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

items: “Do you see children being repeatedly picked on or excluded?”
“Have you ever joined in, laughed, liked or forwarded something that hurt someone?”

blocks.text: Say:
“You are not in trouble for telling me the truth. I want to understand what happens in your world.”
If the child discloses harm, listen first and check safety before beginning a lecture.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: When a child is targeted, parents often want to remove the pain immediately—or make the people responsible feel the same pain. Your child needs something steadier than fury. They need you to say:
“This should not have happened.”
“You did not deserve it.”
“We will act.”
If your child caused harm, they need steadiness too. Not denial. Not rescue from every consequence. Not a permanent label. They need an adult strong enough to say:
“I will not abandon you, and I will not excuse this.”

pages.chapterTitle: Bullying, Cruelty and Online Harm

blocks.text: Safety, dignity and accountability belong in the same conversation. That is how families help stop cruelty—not only for their own child, but for every child sharing the classroom, playground and digital world around them.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support
pages.epigraph: “Seeking help does not mean that the family has failed. It means the child’s struggle has become too heavy to carry without more hands.”

blocks.text: The Curtains Stayed Closed

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: For several weeks, Sameer’s parents assumed he was tired. He stopped playing football after school. He ate dinner in his room. On weekends, he slept until noon and kept the curtains closed. When his mother asked what was wrong, he replied: “Nothing. I’m just tired.” His father believed he needed discipline. “If you stay in bed all day, of course you’ll feel worse,” he said. “Get up and do something useful.” Then Sameer’s teacher called.
He had stopped submitting work and had written a sentence in an assignment:
“I don’t think anything I do matters.”

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: That evening, his mother sat beside him. She resisted the urge to reassure him too quickly. Instead, she said:

“You have not seemed like yourself for several weeks. You’ve stopped football, you’re sleeping differently, and your teacher is worried. I need to ask you something directly. Have you been thinking about hurting yourself or ending your life?”
Sameer began to cry. His mother had feared that asking the question might make things worse. Instead, it opened the door he had been unable to open alone.
A Difficult Time or Something More?

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Children and teenagers experience ordinary emotional distress. They may become upset after:
Not every difficult feeling requires treatment. Look at three things:
Duration
Has the change lasted days, several weeks or months?

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Intensity
Is the reaction proportionate and manageable, or overwhelming?
Interference
Is it affecting:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: The National Institute of Mental Health advises seeking professional guidance when troubling signs persist for weeks or months and interfere with life at home, school or with friends. [NIMH: Child and Adolescent Mental Health](https://www.nimh.nih.gov/health/topics/child-and-adolescent-mental-health) Parents do not need to wait for certainty. Concern is enough reason to begin a conversation.
Signs Worth Noticing
A child may need additional support when you observe persistent or significant changes such as:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Emotional
Behavioural

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: Loss of interest in previously enjoyed activities

blocks.text: Physical

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Academic and social

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: One sign does not automatically indicate a mental-health condition. Patterns matter. So does your knowledge of the child.
Mental Distress Does Not Always Look Like Sadness
Depression may appear as:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Anxiety may appear as:
Distress can also hide behind:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Do not assume a functioning child is a comfortable child.
The NOTICE Conversation
N — Name What You Have Observed
Use facts rather than diagnosis.
“You’ve stopped meeting your friends and have been sleeping much more.”
Not:
“You’re depressed.”

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: O — Offer a Calm Opening
“I’m not here to punish or lecture you. I want to understand.”
Choose a private time without rushing.
T — Take the Child Seriously
Avoid:

items: “You have nothing to be unhappy about.”

blocks.text: A child can have a loving family and still struggle.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: I — Inquire Directly About Safety
If you are concerned, ask clearly:
“Have you been hurting yourself?”
“Have you wished you could go to sleep and not wake up?”
“Are you thinking about suicide?”

Asking about suicide does not put the idea into a child’s mind. Direct questions can help identify danger and open an honest conversation. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Teen Suicide Risk](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/healthy-living/emotional-wellness/Pages/teen-suicide-risk-what-parents-need-to-know.aspx)

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: C — Connect to Help
“We do not have to understand everything before asking someone qualified to help us.”
E — Explain What Happens Next
Tell the child:

items: Why immediate safety may require adult involvement

blocks.text: Avoid secret arrangements when safety is involved.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: If Your Child Says, “No”
Do not automatically assume the concern is over. Say:
“Thank you for answering. I may still arrange some support because the changes I’m seeing are significant.”
A child may deny difficulty because they:

items: Do not yet understand what they feel

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Keep the door open. Continue observing. Seek professional advice based on the signs you see.
Finding the Right Professional
A starting point may be:
Different professionals have different roles.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

rows: Initial assessment, physical-health review and referral
Medical diagnosis, treatment planning and medication where appropriate

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

rows: Structured emotional or behavioural support within their training

blocks.text: Check:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: Experience with the child’s age and concern

blocks.text: Be cautious of anyone promising a guaranteed cure or discouraging appropriate medical care.
Confidentiality and the Parent’s Role

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Older children need some privacy in therapy. Without it, they may not speak honestly. Parents also need enough information to protect health and safety. Before treatment begins, ask the professional to explain:

items: What must be shared if safety is at risk
How parents will receive general progress updates
What to do between appointments if risk increases

blocks.text: Do not demand a complete account of every session. Ask instead:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: “What can we do at home to support the treatment plan?”
Confidentiality should never prevent action when there is serious risk of harm.
If Your Child Resists Help
A child may say:
Respond:
“Support is not a punishment, and you do not have to be ‘crazy’ to receive it. Something is making everyday life harder, and we need more help understanding it.”

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Offer appropriate choice:

items: In-person or online, if clinically suitable
Male or female professional where possible
Parent joining the beginning of the first session

blocks.text: Do not promise that the first professional will be the perfect fit. Sometimes families need to try another qualified provider. When immediate safety is at risk, the child’s refusal cannot be the only deciding factor.
Self-Harm

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Self-harm means deliberately injuring oneself. It does not always mean the child intends to die. It must still be taken seriously because ongoing self-harm is associated with greater suicide risk. Possible signs include:

items: Covering arms or legs in hot weather
Shame or secrecy around the body

blocks.text: Ask calmly:
“I noticed these injuries. Did you make them yourself?”
If yes:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: “Thank you for telling me. Are you thinking about suicide, or were you trying to cope with something unbearable?”
Do not:

items: Demand an immediate promise never to do it again
Ignore the behaviour because the child denies suicidal intent

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Contact an appropriate healthcare or mental-health professional promptly. [American Academy of Pediatrics: When Children and Teens Self-Harm](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/health-issues/conditions/emotional-problems/Pages/when-children-and-teens-self-harm.aspx)
When Suicide May Be a Risk
Warning signs may include:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Every statement about suicide should be taken seriously. Ask:
“Are you thinking about suicide?”

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: If yes, ask:
“Have you made a plan?”
“Do you have access to what you would use?”
“Are you in danger of acting now?”
You are not conducting a full clinical assessment. You are deciding whether urgent help is needed.
If There Is Immediate Risk
If your child has a plan, access to a method, has already harmed themselves, cannot commit to immediate safety, or you believe danger is imminent:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: Remove access to medicines, weapons, toxic substances and other likely means where this can be done safely.
Seek urgent emergency or psychiatric help.
Call emergency services or go to the nearest emergency department.
Do not argue, threaten or lecture.

blocks.text: In India:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: The Government of India lists Tele-MANAS as a mental-health service accessible through 14416 and 1800-891-4416 . [Government of India: National Mental Health Programme](https://www.dghs.mohfw.gov.in/national-mental-health-programme.php) These details must be verified again immediately before publication. A helpline can support and guide. It does not replace emergency medical care when danger is immediate.

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: A Safety Plan
A qualified professional should help create a personalised safety plan. It may include:

items: Warning signs that a crisis is beginning
Coping actions the child can try
Safe people or places that provide distraction
Trusted adults the child can contact
Steps for reducing access to dangerous means
A clear plan for supervision and follow-up

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: A vague “promise not to do anything” is not an adequate safety plan.
Supporting Treatment at Home
Parents can help by:

items: Watching for side effects if medication is prescribed

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: Avoiding making the diagnosis the child’s identity
Asking what helps rather than guessing
Caring for their own emotional health

blocks.text: Recovery may be uneven. A difficult day does not mean treatment has failed. A good day does not necessarily mean support can stop. Review changes with the treating professional.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: Waiting for the child to request help
Treating mental illness as weak character
Sharing the child’s diagnosis widely without need
Expecting medication alone to solve every problem

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: Assuming high achievement means good mental health
Ignoring the parent’s own need for support

blocks.text: Your Support Record
Changes we have observed
How long they have lasted
Areas affected

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: What the child has told us
Safety concerns
Professional contacted

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Appointment date
Immediate safety plan
Follow-up date
Five Sentences to Practise

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

items: “You do not have to protect me from knowing how difficult this feels.”
“I need to ask directly: are you thinking about hurting yourself or ending your life?”
“Support is not a punishment, and seeking it is not a family failure.”
“I will respect your privacy, but I cannot keep secrets about immediate danger.”
“We will take the next step together; you do not have to solve everything tonight.”

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Parents do not need to diagnose their child. They need to notice, listen, protect and connect the child with qualified help. Early support can prevent distress from becoming more severe.
Try This Today
Ask your child:
“If you were having a very difficult time, which three adults could you contact?”
Write down:

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: Save the numbers in the child’s phone where appropriate. A support plan is easier to use when it exists before the crisis.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: It can be frightening to realise that love alone is not relieving your child’s pain. But professional help does not replace your love. It gives that love more tools. Your child still needs your familiar voice, your presence and the ordinary routines that remind them life continues. They also may need knowledge and care that a family cannot provide by itself. There is no shame in opening that door. Sometimes the bravest thing a parent says is:
“I do not fully understand what is happening yet. But I see that you are struggling, I take it seriously, and we are going to find help.”

pages.chapterTitle: When Your Child May Need More Support

blocks.text: That is not helplessness. That is protection.

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness
pages.epigraph: “When adults speak about the changing body with calm and respect, children learn that growing up is not something they need to hide.”

blocks.text: The Stain on the Uniform

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Meera was eleven when her first period began at school. She had heard girls whispering about periods, but no one had properly explained what would happen. When she saw blood on her uniform, she thought she had injured herself. A classmate noticed the stain and laughed. Meera tied a sweater around her waist and spent the rest of the day trying not to move. At home, she hid the uniform. Her mother discovered it later and asked why she had said nothing.

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: “I thought you would be angry,” Meera whispered. Her mother felt a wave of regret. She had planned to discuss menstruation “when the time came.” The time had arrived before the conversation. That evening, they talked. Her mother explained what a period was, showed her how to use a menstrual product and helped her prepare a small school kit. Then she said:
“Nothing dirty or shameful happened to you. Your body began a normal process, and I should have prepared you sooner.”

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: The conversation was a little awkward. It was also deeply relieving. Children do not need parents who never feel awkward. They need parents who are willing to speak despite the awkwardness.
What Is Puberty?
Puberty is the stage during which a child’s body develops towards physical and sexual maturity. Hormones guide changes in:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness
Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Puberty does not begin at exactly the same age or follow the same sequence for every child. Some develop earlier. Some develop later. Children within the same class may look several years apart physically while being the same age. That variation can create embarrassment and comparison even when development is medically typical. The NHS describes a broad normal range, with puberty commonly beginning between approximately ages eight and fourteen. [NHS: Early or Delayed Puberty](https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/early-or-delayed-puberty/)
Parents should use age ranges as guidance, not as a tool for comparing children.

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Prepare Before the Changes Begin
Do not wait for:

items: The first question about body hair
The first cruel comment from a classmate

blocks.text: Begin with simple, accurate conversations before the child needs the information. A younger child may need to know:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Body hair and odour are normal.
Menstruation is a normal body process.
Some changes occur at different times for different people.
Private parts deserve correct names, privacy and care.

blocks.text: Later conversations can add greater detail. One calm conversation every few months is often more useful than one enormous “puberty talk.”

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Use Clear Language
Correct anatomical words reduce confusion and shame. Useful terms include:
A parent does not need to sound clinical. They need to be accurate. Avoid teaching children that ordinary body functions are:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Something only one parent or gender may discuss

blocks.text: Privacy is healthy. Shame is different. Privacy says:
“This is personal, and you decide who may know or see.”
Shame says:
“This part of you is wrong.”
Common Physical Changes
Children may experience changes in different orders.
Changes many children experience

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Changes associated with ovaries and a uterus

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Changes associated with testicles and a penis

items: Enlargement of the testicles and penis
Nocturnal emissions, sometimes called wet dreams

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Not every child’s body or identity fits neatly into common descriptions. Some children have variations in sex development, and some may experience questions about gender as they grow. Parents do not need to have every answer immediately. They do need to keep the conversation respectful and seek suitable medical guidance when development or health raises concern.
Menstruation Without Fear

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Every child should understand the basics of menstruation—not only children who will experience it. This reduces teasing and prepares siblings and classmates to respond respectfully. Explain simply:
“During the menstrual cycle, the lining inside the uterus builds up. When pregnancy does not occur, that lining leaves the body through the vagina as blood and tissue. This is called a period.”
Early cycles may be irregular while the body adjusts. A period kit may contain:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Pads or another suitable menstrual product

blocks.text: Teach:

items: How to use and change the chosen product
How to wrap and dispose of it appropriately
What to do if bleeding begins at school
That ordinary activity can usually continue

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: UNICEF recommends discussing menstruation before the first period, using factual language and making sure children understand products, hygiene and support. [UNICEF: Talking About Periods at Home](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/health/talking-about-periods-at-home)
When period symptoms deserve medical advice
Consult an appropriate healthcare professional if there is:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Pain severe enough to repeatedly prevent school or ordinary activity
No menstruation despite expected pubertal development
Any concern about pregnancy or sexual harm

blocks.text: Do not normalise severe suffering simply because periods can be uncomfortable.

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Erections, Ejaculation and Nocturnal Emissions
Children should know that erections may occur:

They are normal body responses and not evidence of bad behaviour. Nocturnal emissions occur when semen is released during sleep. They may begin during puberty and do not require treatment. Prepare the child practically:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Place laundry in the usual place.

blocks.text: Avoid joking, announcing it to relatives or treating the event as proof that the child is now fully adult. Physical maturity and emotional maturity are not the same.
Masturbation and Privacy

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Many young people discover that touching their own body can feel pleasurable. Parents may have personal, religious or cultural values around this subject. Whatever those values, avoid humiliation or frightening myths. Teach:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Private behaviour belongs in a private place.
No one should be involved without lawful, age-appropriate consent.
Private images should never be requested, created or shared.
The behaviour should not injure the body or interfere with school, sleep, relationships or ordinary life.
Questions about pain, compulsive behaviour or distress can be discussed with a healthcare professional.

blocks.text: The key lesson is bodily privacy and responsibility—not shame.

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Hygiene Without Humiliation
Puberty may require new routines:
Avoid public comments such as:
“You smell terrible.”
Say privately:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: “Your body is producing more sweat now. That is normal during puberty, and it means your hygiene routine needs to change.”
Provide:

items: Reminders while the routine is developing

blocks.text: Do not assume children automatically know what to do.
Acne and Appearance
Acne is common during puberty and can affect confidence. Helpful basics include:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Not repeatedly picking or squeezing spots
Seeking medical advice for painful, severe, scarring or distressing acne

blocks.text: Do not blame acne on being dirty. Avoid pointing it out every time you see the child. What is visible to you has probably already occupied much of their attention.
Puberty and Emotion

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Puberty may bring stronger self-consciousness, fluctuating emotions and sensitivity to other people’s opinions. But do not explain every difficulty with:
“It’s just hormones.”
A child may also be facing:
Listen to the actual concern. Development provides context. It should not be used to dismiss suffering.

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Body Image
Children may compare:
Parents can help by:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Speaking respectfully about their own body
Keeping food and movement free from shame

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: UNICEF advises seeking help when appearance worries begin controlling eating, exercise, social participation or daily life. [UNICEF: Teens and Body Image](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/mental-health/teens-body-image)
Privacy Changes During Puberty
Growing children may want:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: To knock before entering bedrooms or bathrooms
To attend part of a medical appointment without a parent
Privacy around periods or nocturnal emissions

blocks.text: Respect these needs where safety permits. Teach the same respect in return:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Do not borrow personal items without asking.
Do not photograph people changing or sleeping.
Never share private body information as a joke.
Seek help if anyone violates bodily privacy.

blocks.text: Privacy supports dignity. It does not mean parents abandon supervision or ignore danger.
How to Begin the Conversation
Try:
“You may notice changes in your body over the next few years. I want you to have accurate information before anything surprises you.”
Or:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: “What has school taught you about puberty? Was anything confusing?”
Or:
“You don’t have to ask every question aloud. You can write it or send it to me.”
If the child says, “This is awkward,” answer:
“It is a little awkward. We can still talk about it.”
If you do not know:
“I’m not sure. Let’s find a reliable answer.”
Credibility matters more than pretending expertise.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Waiting until puberty has already begun
Using nicknames because correct words feel uncomfortable
Teaching only boys about erections and wet dreams
Discussing the child’s body with relatives

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Treating sexual curiosity as moral failure
Invading privacy without a safety reason

blocks.text: When to Seek Medical Advice
Speak with a paediatrician or another qualified healthcare professional when:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Signs of puberty appear unusually early
Period pain or bleeding is severe
There is a breast, genital or testicular concern
There is persistent pain, unusual discharge, sores or injury
Growth or weight changes are concerning

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: Body distress interferes with everyday life
The child is distressed about development
You suspect sexual abuse or exploitation

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics advises medical review when girls show no signs of puberty by approximately thirteen or boys by approximately fourteen, and when puberty begins unusually early. Individual circumstances vary, so concerns should be discussed with the child’s clinician. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Physical Changes During Puberty](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/gradeschool/puberty/Pages/Physical-Development-of-School-Age-Children.aspx)
The Puberty Preparation Checklist
Does my child know:

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: That puberty begins at different times?
How to use menstrual products if needed?
What erections and wet dreams are?
That body hair and odour are normal?
That private behaviour belongs in private?

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

items: That no one may demand private images?
When a health concern should be reported?

blocks.text: Products or support we need to arrange
Questions my child has
Trusted health professional

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Five Sentences to Practise

items: “Your body is changing normally, and you can ask me anything.”
“Different children begin puberty at different times.”
“Periods, erections and wet dreams are body processes—not reasons for shame.”
“Your body is private, and you deserve respect.”
“I don’t know the answer yet, but I will help you find a reliable one.”

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Puberty education is not one conversation about reproduction. It is ongoing guidance about:
Try This Today
Ask:
“What is one thing about puberty that children your age often misunderstand?”

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Listen before correcting. Then offer one simple fact. Keep the conversation short enough that another conversation can follow. Children are more likely to return when every question does not become a lecture.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Puberty Without Shame or Awkwardness

blocks.text: Your child may roll their eyes. They may ask you to stop talking. They may appear deeply embarrassed. Do not mistake embarrassment for lack of need. Behind the awkwardness is a child trying to understand a body that may be changing faster than their confidence. Speak calmly. Protect privacy. Answer honestly. And never make their changing body family entertainment. The goal is not to remove every awkward moment. It is to ensure that awkwardness never becomes silence—and that silence never leaves your child alone with fear, misinformation or shame.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety
pages.epigraph: “A child who knows that love never requires fear, secrecy or pressure is better prepared to recognise when something is wrong.”

blocks.text: “But He Said I Agreed”
Sixteen-year-old Diya had been talking to a boy from school. At first, the messages were friendly. Then he began asking for private photographs. Diya said no. He replied:
“Don’t you trust me?”

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: When she refused again, he stopped speaking to her. Two days later, he apologised and promised he would never share anything. Diya eventually sent one image. A few weeks later, when she tried to end the relationship, he threatened to forward it. Diya felt trapped. She did not tell her mother because she expected to hear:
“Why did you send it?”
When her mother finally discovered what had happened, she did ask that question—but stopped herself before saying it aloud. Instead, she said:
“Sending it was risky, and we will talk about that. Threatening you is still wrong. We will deal with your safety first.”

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Diya needed accountability. She also needed protection. One did not cancel the other.
Sexual-Safety Education Begins Long Before Dating
Consent is not a single teenage conversation about sex. Children begin learning it through ordinary family life. They learn when adults:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Allow a child to refuse unwanted affection
Teach correct names for body parts
Require children to respect other people’s boundaries

blocks.text: A child who is forced to hug every relative may learn:
“Politeness matters more than what my body is telling me.”
Teach instead:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: “You may greet respectfully without giving physical affection.”

A child’s refusal does not control every health or safety decision. They may still need an examination, vaccination or hygiene care. But adults should explain what is happening, preserve dignity and involve the child as much as possible.
What Consent Means
Consent means freely and clearly agreeing to a particular action. Healthy consent is:
Freely given
No threats, guilt, blackmail, manipulation or misuse of authority.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Informed
The person understands what they are agreeing to.
Specific
Agreement to one action is not agreement to everything.
Reversible
A person can change their mind.
Ongoing
Consent must continue throughout an interaction.
Mutual

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Each person’s comfort and boundaries matter. Silence is not automatically consent. Freezing is not consent. Past agreement is not permanent consent. A relationship does not create ownership. Consent cannot be obtained from someone who is unconscious, severely impaired by alcohol or drugs, or otherwise unable to make a free and informed decision. The American Academy of Pediatrics explains that consent requires an unpressured yes, applies to each specific action and can be withdrawn. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Helping Teens Make Choices About Sex](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/teen/dating-sex/Pages/Helping-Teens-Resist-Sexual-Pressure.aspx)

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Consent and Indian Child-Protection Law

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Parents should distinguish between teaching the ethical skill of consent and understanding the law. Under India’s Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012—commonly called the POCSO Act—a child is any person below eighteen years of age . The Act addresses sexual assault, sexual harassment and the use of children in sexual material. [India Code: POCSO Act](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/handle/123456789/2079) This means that a teenager’s description of an interaction as “consensual” does not remove the child-protection and reporting implications that may apply under Indian law.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: POCSO also contains reporting obligations where a person knows or reasonably apprehends that an offence has occurred or may occur. [India Code: POCSO Section 19](https://www.indiacode.nic.in/show-data?abv=CG&actid=AC_CEN_13_14_00005_201232_1517807323686&orderno=19&orgactid=AC_CG_61_334_00060_00060_1591080455267&statehandle=123456789%2F2490) Individual situations can be legally complex, especially when adolescents of similar age are involved.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: This book cannot provide case-specific legal advice. Families, schools and professionals should seek qualified guidance and follow applicable reporting duties rather than making assumptions.
Healthy and Unhealthy Relationships

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

rows: Both people may have other friends
Passwords are demanded as proof of trust

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

rows: One person uses fear or humiliation
Gifts or attention are treated as payment for intimacy
Both people can change their mind
Past consent is used as permanent permission

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

rows: Threats, stalking or blackmail follow conflict
One person controls clothing, location or communication
Risk is treated as proof of love

blocks.text: Teach your child to ask:
“Can I say no and still feel safe?”
That question reveals a great deal about a relationship.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Pressure Does Not Always Sound Threatening
Sexual pressure may sound affectionate:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Teach:
Love is not proved by surrendering a boundary.
A gift does not purchase access to someone’s body.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Another person’s feelings do not create a sexual obligation.
Threatening self-harm to prevent someone leaving is coercive and requires adult help.
The CLEAR Consent Check
C — Can I Choose Freely?
Would I make the same choice without fear, guilt or pressure?
L — Legal and Age-Appropriate?
Does the situation comply with child-protection law and family safety expectations?

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: E — Each Step Is Specific
Have both people agreed to this particular action?
A — Able to Think Clearly
Is everyone conscious, informed and unimpaired?
R — Reversible
Can either person stop without punishment? If any part is missing, proceed no further.
Talking About Sexual Feelings and Choices
Parents may hold strong cultural, moral or religious values. Share them clearly:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: “Our family believes sexual relationships should wait because…”
But also provide factual health and safety information. A child who fears moral judgement may hide exactly the situation in which protection is most needed. Discuss:

items: The right to delay or refuse

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: The CDC recommends repeated, open conversations that include healthy relationships, expectations, pregnancy and STI prevention—not only warnings about consequences. [CDC: Talking With Teens About Sex](https://www.cdc.gov/healthy-youth-parent-resources/conversation-tips/index.html) For individual sexual-health questions, arrange access to a qualified healthcare professional and explain the limits of confidentiality that apply to minors and safety concerns.
Pornography Is Not Education

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Children may encounter pornography accidentally, through peers or through deliberate curiosity. Reacting with shock may end the conversation. Say:
“Curiosity is understandable. Pornography is made for adult entertainment and is not a reliable guide to bodies, consent or healthy relationships.”
Explain that pornography may:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Be illegal when it involves anyone under eighteen

blocks.text: Ask:

items: “Was anyone pressuring you to watch or share it?”
“Do you have questions about what you saw?”

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: If viewing becomes compulsive, distressing, illegal or connected to harmful behaviour, seek professional help.
Private Images, Sexting and Sextortion
A private image can be:
The safest rule for a minor is:
Do not create, request, possess or share sexual images of yourself or another child.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Teach children never to respond to pressure with more images or money. If someone threatens them:

items: Preserve usernames, links, threats and appropriate evidence.
Do not forward the intimate image.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: India’s [National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal](https://www.cybercrime.gov.in/) includes reporting routes for cybercrimes against women and children. Immediate danger can be reported through 112 , and children in crisis can access Child Helpline 1098 .
Grooming
Grooming is a process in which someone builds access, trust and secrecy in order to exploit a child. A person grooming a child may:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Present themselves as the only person who understands
Isolate the child from trusted adults

blocks.text: The person may be:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Teach:
Adults and older people should not ask children to keep relationships, gifts, messages or touching secret from safe caregivers.
A surprise has an ending. A dangerous secret is meant to isolate.
If Your Child Discloses Sexual Harm
Your first response matters.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Say:

items: “I may need to involve people whose job is to protect children.”

blocks.text: Do:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Write down what was said as accurately as possible.

blocks.text: Do not:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Confront the alleged person in a way that increases risk
Make the child repeat the account to many relatives
Delay urgent medical or official help

blocks.text: The purpose of your first conversation is not to investigate. It is to receive the disclosure, protect the child and connect them with qualified help.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: When Your Child Is Accused of Crossing a Boundary
Take the allegation seriously. Do not begin with:
“My child would never do that.”
Ask:

items: Was there pressure, touching, imagery or harassment?
What professional or legal guidance is needed?

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Protect the rights and privacy of all children involved. Do not coach your child to conceal information or attack the reporting child. Say:
“I love you. We need the truth, and we need to make sure everyone is safe.”
A child who has crossed a sexual boundary may need firm limits, assessment, education and professional intervention—not humiliation or denial.
Family Safety Rules
Teach every child:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Other people’s bodies belong to them.
Stop when someone says no or appears uncomfortable.
No one may demand private images.
Do not share another person’s private information or images.
Adults should not ask children to keep sexual secrets.
You may tell a trusted adult even if someone threatened you.

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: If one adult does not help, tell another.
You will not lose your family’s protection because you made a mistake before asking for help.

blocks.text: Common Mistakes

items: Waiting until the child begins dating
Teaching boys only to “control themselves” rather than understand consent

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Assuming abuse is always committed by a stranger
Giving moral rules without health information
Removing all help after a child breaks a rule
Confronting an alleged perpetrator before securing safety
Forcing a child to hug relatives

blocks.text: Urgent Red Flags
Act promptly when there is:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Sudden gifts or money from an unexplained source
Pain, injury, bleeding or pregnancy concern
A child frightened of a particular person

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: Sexualised behaviour that is coercive, harmful or significantly unexpected
Self-harm or suicidal thoughts following sexual pressure or exposure

blocks.text: For immediate danger in India:
Seek qualified medical, child-protection and legal assistance. This chapter is general education, not individual medical or legal advice.
The Family Consent Check

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Does my child understand:

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: A clear yes is different from silence?
One yes does not mean yes to everything?
Intoxicated or unconscious people cannot consent?
Relationship status does not create ownership?
Private images are unsafe and legally serious for minors?
They can call me without first explaining everything?

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Trusted adults my child can contact
Our emergency code word
Healthcare contact
Five Sentences to Practise

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

items: “You never owe anyone access to your body.”
“A person who respects you will respect your no.”
“Do not send more images or money; come to me and we will handle it together.”
“You are not in trouble for telling me someone crossed a sexual boundary.”
“If one adult does not listen, keep telling until someone helps.”

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Sexual-safety education is not permission to take risks. It is protection against secrecy, misinformation and coercion. Children need facts, values, boundaries, healthcare knowledge and a safe route back to their parents.
Try This Today
Ask your child:
“How would you know whether someone’s yes was freely given?”
Listen. Then add:
“A safe yes can be changed to no without fear, guilt or punishment.”
Keep the conversation brief enough that another one can follow.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: Many parents worry that speaking openly will introduce ideas too early. Silence does not keep children uninformed. It leaves friends, algorithms, pornography and people with their own intentions to provide the information instead. Your calm voice belongs in this conversation. You can share your values without replacing facts. You can hold boundaries without creating shame. And you can make one promise your child will remember:
“If you are frightened, pressured or in trouble, come to me. We will protect you first and work through the rest together.”

pages.chapterTitle: Bodies, Relationships, Consent and Sexual Safety

blocks.text: That promise may become one of the most important safety tools your child carries into adolescence.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person
pages.epigraph: “Your child does not become less connected to the family by becoming more clearly themselves.”

blocks.text: The Question at Dinner

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: For years, Arjun had attended every family religious ceremony without complaint. At fifteen, he began asking questions. One evening, he said: “I don’t know whether I believe all of this.” The table became silent. His grandfather looked hurt. His father replied: “This is not something you get to question. These are our family values.” Arjun stopped talking. He still attended the ceremonies, but his questions moved elsewhere—to friends and anonymous online spaces where no one knew him.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: His mother worried that listening would encourage him to reject the family. Then she realised that refusing to listen had not removed the questions. It had only removed the family from them. A few days later, she said:
“You do not have to pretend with me. Tell me what you are wondering. I may disagree, but I want to understand.”

Arjun did not announce a final answer. He was not ready for one. He was trying to work out what he had inherited, what he believed and what kind of person he wanted to become. That work is part of adolescence.
What Is Identity?

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Identity is the developing answer to questions such as:

items: What kind of person do I want to become?
How am I similar to my family?
How do other people see me?
What do I want my life to stand for?

blocks.text: Identity may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person
Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: No single part tells the whole story. A child may feel deeply rooted in family culture while questioning one tradition. They may share a parent’s values but express them differently. They may feel connected to several communities at once. Identity is not a form a teenager completes. It develops through experience, reflection, relationships and time.
Exploration Is Part of Adolescence
Adolescents commonly explore:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Some choices last. Others are temporary experiments. Parents do not have to treat every new preference as a final identity or every disagreement as rebellion. Try:
“What interests you about this?”
“How long have you been thinking about it?”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: “What does it mean to you?”
“Is there anything you’re worried I might say?”

The American Academy of Pediatrics notes that adolescents increasingly develop individuality, question beliefs, explore sexuality and gradually identify their own values. Respectful family relationships remain important throughout this process. [American Academy of Pediatrics: Stages of Adolescence](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/teen/Pages/Stages-of-Adolescence.aspx)
Family Influence Does Not Disappear

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Allowing exploration does not mean parents become silent. Children need adults who share:
The difference is between influence and control.
Influence says:
“This is what our family believes, and here is why.”
Control says:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: “You must say you believe it, whether or not you do.”
Influence says:
“You may ask difficult questions.”
Control says:
“Questions prove disloyalty.”
Influence says:
“Your behaviour must remain respectful.”
Control says:
“Respect means agreeing with me.”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Forced agreement may produce outward compliance. It does not necessarily create conviction. Values become stronger when children understand, examine and choose how to live them.
The VALUES Method
V — Voice the Family’s Values Clearly
Do not assume children know what the family stands for. You might say:
“In our family, we value honesty, dignity, responsibility, compassion and respect.”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Then explain how those values affect decisions. A value that appears only during lectures will not carry much weight.
A — Act as a Model
Children notice whether adults:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

items: Practise the faith or principles they speak about

blocks.text: If the family teaches respect but uses humiliation, the child learns that power outranks values.
L — Listen to the Child’s Reasoning
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

items: “Who might be affected by this choice?”
“Does this match the person you want to become?”
“Would you be comfortable if your decision became public?”
“What might you think about this five years from now?”

blocks.text: Good questions help values become internal rather than merely imposed.
U — Understand Difference Without Removing Limits

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: A teenager may disagree with the parent’s belief and still follow reasonable household rules. For example:
“You do not have to share my religious conviction. While you are attending this family ceremony, respectful behaviour is expected.”
Or:
“You may choose your clothing within our agreed safety and school requirements. I will not mock your style.”
Boundaries should focus on behaviour, safety and responsibility—not forced inner agreement.
E — Expand the Child’s World
Encourage contact with:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Identity becomes more thoughtful when it is built from more than algorithms and peer approval.
S — Stay Connected Through Change
A child should not have to choose between honesty and belonging. Say:
“You may change. We may disagree. We remain family.”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: The CDC recommends supporting adolescents’ growing interests and independence while maintaining regular connection, clear expectations and empathetic listening. [CDC: Encouraging Teen Independence](https://www.cdc.gov/parenting-teens/tips-encouraging-independence/index.html)
Cultural Identity
Cultural identity can provide:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: But children may also question traditions that feel restrictive, unequal or disconnected from their life. Avoid:
“If you reject this custom, you reject us.”
Try:
“This tradition matters to me because it connects me to my parents and childhood. Tell me what feels difficult about it for you.”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Parents can preserve culture by making it alive—not only compulsory. Teach:

items: Which values the tradition is intended to carry

blocks.text: Children are more likely to maintain a culture they understand and experience as meaningful.
Gender Roles
Families sometimes assign identity through expectation:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

items: Certain careers belong to one gender.

blocks.text: These rules limit everyone. Teach all children:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person
Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: UNICEF encourages families to challenge restrictive gender stereotypes and provide children of all genders with equal opportunities, care and freedom to develop interests. [UNICEF: Equal Treatment from Day One](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-development/equal-treatment-gender) A child’s preference for particular clothes, toys or interests does not by itself determine identity or sexuality. Allow interests without forcing conclusions.
Gender Identity and Sexual Orientation
Some children and adolescents may question or share something about:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: A parent may feel surprised, confused, worried or in conflict with personal beliefs. Do not make the child manage the adult’s first emotional reaction. Begin with:
“Thank you for trusting me.”
“How long have you been thinking about this?”
“What does this mean to you right now?”
“Are you safe at school and online?”
“Who else knows?”
You do not need to understand everything immediately. You do need to avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

items: Attempts to frighten the child into silence
Treating the child as contaminated or shameful

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: A child may use a label and later change it. Another may be certain. Another may not want a label. Do not rush the child towards or away from a conclusion. If the child or family needs support, seek a qualified, ethical healthcare or mental-health professional experienced in adolescent development. The purpose should be wellbeing, understanding and safety—not coercion.
When Parents Disagree Deeply
Some differences touch a parent’s core values. The goal is not false agreement. It is safe relationship. A parent can say:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: “I am still processing this, and some of my beliefs are different. I love you, and I will not humiliate or abandon you.”
Take your fear to:

items: Appropriate faith or community adviser who will prioritise the child’s safety

blocks.text: Do not unload it entirely onto the child. The child needs room for their development. The parent needs a separate place for adult processing.
Politics, Social Causes and Strong Opinions

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Teenagers may adopt strong positions. Sometimes this reflects genuine moral thought. Sometimes it reflects peer belonging or online influence. Do not dismiss every view as immaturity. Ask:

items: “Does this source benefit from making you angry?”
“How does this position affect real people?”

blocks.text: Teach intellectual humility:
“Strong values and open thinking can exist together.”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Set boundaries around:
Freedom of thought does not mean freedom from responsibility.
Family Reputation and the Child’s Authenticity
In close communities, parents may worry:
“What will people say?”

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Community consequences can be real. But if reputation becomes the highest family value, children learn to hide problems until they become dangerous. They may conceal:
Teach:
“Our privacy matters, but your safety matters more.”
A family can protect confidentiality without making the child feel like a source of shame.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Let Identity Include Contribution
Identity should not be built only from:
Children also need to know:

items: What can others rely on me for?
What problem do I want to understand?
How do I behave when no one is watching?

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Give teenagers meaningful roles. Contribution turns identity from:
“How do people see me?”
towards:
“What kind of person am I becoming?”
Common Mistakes
1. Treating difference as rejection
Your child can love the family without becoming identical to it.
2. Demanding immediate certainty
Identity may develop gradually.
3. Turning every preference into a label

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Allow interests and expression to breathe.
4. Mocking the child’s stage
Temporary does not mean meaningless.
5. Forcing disclosure
A child owns private information unless safety or legal duties require adult action.
6. Using financial dependence as emotional control
Providing for a minor should not be conditional on ideological agreement.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: 7. Allowing online spaces to provide all guidance
Remain part of the conversation and teach source evaluation.
8. Mistaking obedience for values
A child may comply without developing judgement.
9. Making the parent’s fear the child’s responsibility
Process adult emotions with adults.
10. Removing boundaries to appear accepting
Respect and safety expectations still apply.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Red Flags
Seek qualified help when identity-related distress is accompanied by:

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

items: Coercive attempts to change the child
Threats from peers or community members

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: If the child may be unsafe at home, school or in the community, involve appropriate child-protection or emergency services.
Your Family Values Map
Five values we want our home to practise

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: What each value looks like in adult behaviour
What it looks like in a child’s behaviour
Traditions we want to preserve
Why they matter

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Traditions or expectations we may need to examine
Differences we can allow
Boundaries that remain necessary
How we will keep disagreement respectful

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Five Sentences to Practise

items: “You may question an idea without disrespecting the people who hold it.”
“Tell me what this means to you before I respond.”
“Our family values should be visible in how we treat people—not only in what we say.”
“You do not have to decide your entire identity today.”

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: The goal is not to create a child who repeats the family’s words. It is to raise a young person who can think, belong, choose and act with integrity. Parents provide roots through:
They provide room through:
Children need both.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: Try This Today
Ask your child:
“Which value do you think our family practises well—and which one do we talk about more than we practise?”
Do not defend the family immediately. Listen for the gap your child sees. Their answer may reveal an opportunity for the entire household to grow.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: There is a moment in parenting when the child begins to hold the family up to the values it taught them. That moment can feel disrespectful. Sometimes it is. Sometimes it is also evidence that the child was listening. They are testing ideas. They are noticing contradictions. They are deciding what they will carry into adulthood. You may not recognise every choice. You may grieve some differences. But your child’s distinctness is not proof that your influence disappeared.

pages.chapterTitle: Identity, Values and Becoming Their Own Person

blocks.text: A well-rooted child does not remain beside the tree forever. They carry something of its shelter into places the tree itself can never go. Stay available. Speak honestly. Hold necessary boundaries. And make your relationship strong enough to contain both belonging and difference.

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility
pages.epigraph: “Our children do not need us to fear their digital world. They need us to help them navigate it.”

blocks.text: When the Phone Became More Than a Phone

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: When Kavita gave her thirteen-year-old son his first phone, the reason was simple. His school bus route had changed, and she wanted him to be able to call if he was delayed. At first, the phone stayed in his schoolbag. Then came the class messaging group. A few games. Short videos. Messages from friends late at night. Within months, Kavita noticed that he was sleeping later, rushing through homework and becoming angry whenever she asked him to put the phone away.

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: One evening, after discovering him online well past midnight, she took the phone and announced: “You’re not getting this back for a month.” Her son shouted that she did not trust him. The next day, he began using an old device without telling her. The punishment had removed the phone from her sight. It had not taught him how to manage it. That was the moment Kavita realised that her real task was not simply to control a device. It was to help her son develop judgement.

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: They started again. Together, they discussed where the phone would sleep at night, which apps required permission, what he should do if someone made him uncomfortable and how trust could earn greater freedom. The arrangement was not perfect. There were still disagreements. But the phone gradually stopped being a battleground and became something they were learning to manage together. That is the goal of digital parenting. Not perfect control.
Not unlimited freedom. Gradual responsibility.
Your Child’s Digital Life Is Real Life

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: Adults sometimes describe online life as though it is separate from “real life.” For a growing child, that distinction may not exist. Friendships continue through messages. Homework arrives through school platforms. Games become meeting places. Music, humour, fashion and news travel through social media. A child may learn a language, discover an interest or speak to a distant grandparent through a screen. Technology can support:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: It can also expose children to:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: The answer is not to describe every screen as harmful. It is to help children recognise what technology is doing for them and what it may be doing to them .

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: Look Beyond the Number of Hours

Screen time matters, but the number alone rarely tells the whole story. Two children may each spend two hours online. One may be creating music with friends. The other may be scrolling through content that leaves her anxious and ashamed of her body. The hours are the same. The experience is not. The American Academy of Pediatrics offers a useful framework called the 5 Cs of Media Use :

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

rows: How does this particular child respond to media?
What is the child watching, playing or creating?
Is technology becoming the only way the child can manage discomfort?

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

rows: What healthy activity is media replacing?
Are adults and children talking openly about digital life?

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: This approach moves the conversation beyond a universal time limit and towards the needs of the individual child. [American Academy of Pediatrics: The 5 Cs of Media Use](https://www.aap.org/en/patient-care/media-and-children/center-of-excellence-on-social-media-and-youth-mental-health/5cs-of-media-use/) Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

items: Does my child still enjoy offline interests?
Can my child stop without a major struggle?
Does online activity usually leave my child energised, neutral or distressed?

blocks.text: A screen habit needs attention when it repeatedly displaces something essential.
Is Your Child Ready for a Phone?

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: There is no single age at which every child becomes ready. A phone may be needed for transport, school or family communication before a child is ready for unrestricted internet access. These do not have to arrive together. Before providing a smartphone or allowing a social-media account, consider:
Practical readiness
Can the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

items: Charge and care for a device?
Tell an adult when something goes wrong?

blocks.text: Emotional readiness
Can the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

items: Resist at least some peer pressure?
Understand that online popularity is not the same as personal worth?
Ask for help without believing the device will automatically be taken away?

blocks.text: Safety readiness
Does the child understand:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

items: What personal information must remain private?
Why strangers online may not be who they claim to be?
That images can be copied and redistributed?
How to block and report an account?
Why suspicious links should not be opened?
That threats, sexual requests and requests for secrecy must be shown to an adult?

blocks.text: Readiness does not mean the child will never make a mistake. It means there is enough foundation for supervised practice.

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: Build a Family Digital Agreement
Rules work better when they are clear before a problem occurs. A family digital agreement should describe access, safety and responsibility—not merely hours.

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

rows: Phones stay away during family meals
Homework is completed without entertainment notifications

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

rows: New apps require discussion or permission
Accounts begin with the safest suitable settings
Unknown people are not accepted without checking

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

rows: Live location is not publicly shared
No private, intimate or humiliating images are created or forwarded

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

rows: The child can report a mistake without immediate shaming
The agreement is reviewed as maturity grows

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: The rules should apply to adults where appropriate. It is difficult to teach children that meals are phone-free while answering every notification at the table. It is equally difficult to teach privacy while posting their embarrassing moments without permission. Parents are part of the family’s digital culture.
Protect Sleep First

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: A phone in the bedroom can disturb sleep even when a child intends to use it responsibly. There is always one more message. One more video. One more level. Notifications can interrupt sleep, while emotionally stimulating content can make it harder to settle. A simple family practice is to create a shared charging area outside bedrooms. This avoids turning bedtime into a nightly negotiation. You might say:
“This is not because we believe you are irresponsible. Sleep protects your mood, learning and health. All devices rest outside bedrooms—including ours.”

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: If a child needs an alarm, provide an ordinary alarm clock. A boundary is easier to maintain when the practical excuse has already been solved.
Social Media: Teach the Machinery Behind the Screen

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

blocks.text: Children often assume that their feed simply shows what is popular or important. Explain that platforms may select content according to what keeps a person watching, clicking or reacting. A child who pauses over one worrying video may soon receive many similar videos. Repetition can make extreme, misleading or unhealthy ideas appear normal. Teach your child to ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Phones, Social Media, Gaming and Digital Responsibility

items: What do they want me to feel?
Are they informing me, entertaining me or selling to me?
Could this image or video have been altered?
Why might the platform be showing me more of this?
How do I feel after viewing it?
